In short, a REET presents an opportunity for an individual that doesn't want to do the work to invest in large scale income producing real estate deals like office buildings, shopping malls, hotels, resorts, cell storage, facilities, warehouses and yes, my favorite, the apartment complex. In fact, you could invest in a REET that had all those things in one REET in a bunch of different areas or locations in the country. A REET doesn't invest in properties by the way to resell them. There are two main types of REETs, equity REETs and mortgage REETs. An equity REET invest in a property, a mortgage REET and you're investing in the debt. So equity REETs own and operate income producing real estate. Commercial REETs would invest in properties such as shopping malls or office space. Residential REETs on the other hand would be the apartment complex or student housing that we focus on. In general, equity REETs are the standard method for investing in the market for those who don't want to do it themselves. The alternative, mortgage REETs aren't as common but they're always an option. This is somebody that doesn't want to lend their own money out but they want to be on the debt side of making money. These REETs focus on the investment ownership of property, mortgages and revenues generated by the interest earned on the loans and the fees associated. In many cases, they have a bit more risk due to their sensitivity with interest rate fluctuations and their dependence upon the stock market itself. As the value of the stock goes up and down every day, so does the value of your REET, which means the short term performance of a REET can be slow. It can go down. It could pop up a little bit.
